summary_quote: Only one smoke test exists for the entire ML pipeline. Reproducibility (the primary success criterion) is completely untested, the training loop has zero coverage, and the data split gap has no regression fixture. Three critical gaps against explicit project goals.

findings.title: Training loop, optimizer step, and loss computation are entirely untested
findings.explanation: The entire train() function — config loading, dataset construction, DataLoader, optimizer loop, loss computation, and final print — has no test of any kind. The one test in the suite (test_model_constructs) only constructs an MLP and asserts is not None; it never touches train.py. If train() crashes, produces NaN loss, or silently exits before completing all epochs, no test will catch it.
findings.suggestion: Add a fast integration test that calls train() with a minimal in-memory config (e.g., 2 epochs, batch_size=32, 200 samples) and asserts: (1) the function completes without raising; (2) the returned or captured loss for epoch 2 is finite (not NaN/Inf); (3) model parameters have changed from their initialized values.

findings.title: Reproducibility — the primary success criterion — has no test
findings.explanation: The aims snapshot lists reproducibility as the top success criterion: 'two runs with the same config produce identical loss curves.' The training loop uses DataLoader(shuffle=True) with no seed set, and TabularDataset.__init__ calls np.random.randn and np.random.randint with no fixed seed. There is no test that runs two training calls with the same seed and asserts matching loss values.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that: (1) sets torch.manual_seed(42), np.random.seed(42), and passes generator=torch.Generator().manual_seed(42) to the DataLoader; (2) runs two training calls with identical config; (3) asserts the per-epoch loss arrays are equal to within floating-point tolerance.

findings.title: Data split absence has no test; train/val/test correctness is unverified
findings.explanation: load_full_dataset() returns the entire dataset with no split. The aims snapshot lists 'train/val/test split with no leakage' as a success criterion. There is no test asserting that split indices are disjoint, that validation loss is computed on held-out samples, or even that a split function exists.
findings.suggestion: When the split is implemented, add a parameterized test for split_dataset() asserting: (1) len(train) + len(val) + len(test) == len(full); (2) the index sets are pairwise disjoint; (3) no sample appears in both train and test.

findings.title: The only test asserts `is not None` — it proves nothing about model behavior
findings.explanation: assert m is not None will pass as long as the constructor does not raise. It does not verify output shape, that the forward pass produces logits of the correct dimension, or that predict() returns class indices in the expected range.
findings.suggestion: Expand the existing test to: (1) run a forward pass with a batch of the correct input shape and assert output.shape == (batch_size, output_dim); (2) call predict(m, x) and assert the result has shape (batch_size,) and values in [0, output_dim).

findings.title: No test for non-determinism in TabularDataset: fresh instantiation yields different data each call
findings.explanation: TabularDataset.__init__ calls np.random.randn and np.random.randint without a seed. Two instantiations produce different samples. This is a flakiness vector for any test that constructs the dataset without first setting a seed.
findings.suggestion: Add an optional seed parameter to TabularDataset.__init__. Tests that construct datasets should pass a fixed seed (TabularDataset(seed=0)) so their data is deterministic.

stage_handoff_notes: The data leakage / missing split issue is a joint concern: from this lens it is a missing test; the ML/data integrity lens should also raise it as a pipeline correctness violation. The np.random and torch.manual_seed absence in train.py is both a reproducibility test gap (mine) and potentially an ML-correctness gap (ML persona). No security, architecture, or performance findings were surfaced — those belong to specialist personas.
